C:
Recommendation: use webhooks as the primary mechanism, with a lightweight polling job as a reconciliation backstop. Pure polling only if the third party doesn't offer webhooks or you can't expose a public endpoint.

Reasoning:

**Why webhooks win as the default.** They push data the moment something changes, so you get near-real-time updates without burning requests. Polling forces a trade-off you can't escape: poll often and you waste calls (mostly returning "nothing changed," eating rate limits and cost), or poll rarely and you accept latency. Webhooks sidestep that entirely — you do work only when there's actually work to do. For most integrations (payments, CRM updates, deploy events, order status), this is the right shape.

**Why you still keep polling around.** Webhooks are fire-and-forget from the provider's side, so they fail silently — a dropped delivery, your endpoint being down for a deploy, or a network blip means you simply never hear about an event. A low-frequency reconciliation poll (e.g. "fetch anything updated in the last N minutes" every few minutes, or hourly) catches what webhooks missed. This belt-and-suspenders pattern is what mature integrations actually run.

**When to skip webhooks and just poll:**
- The provider doesn't offer them.
- You can't expose a public HTTPS endpoint (locked-down internal network, serverless cold-start concerns, no easy ingress).
- Event volume is tiny and latency genuinely doesn't matter — a daily batch sync is simpler than standing up endpoint + signature verification + retry handling.
- You need a guaranteed-complete snapshot rather than a stream of deltas.

**The real cost of webhooks** — budget for these or they'll bite you: you must verify signatures (treat the endpoint as attacker-reachable), handle retries and duplicate deliveries (make processing idempotent), respond fast and process async (don't do heavy work in the request handler or the provider will time out and retry), and handle out-of-order arrival.

The two decision factors that would change my answer: does the provider support webhooks at all, and can you reliably expose an endpoint? If both are yes, go webhooks-primary. If either is no, poll. If you tell me the specific integration and your latency tolerance, I'll firm this up.

Webhooks sidestep that entirely — you do work only when there's actually work to do. For most integrations (payments, CRM updates, deploy events, order status), this is the right shape.

**Why you still keep polling around.** Webhooks are fire-and-forget from the provider's side, so they fail silently — a dropped delivery, your endpoint being down for a deploy, or a network blip means you simply never hear about an event. A low-frequency reconciliation poll (e.g. "fetch anything updated in the last N minutes" every few minutes, or hourly) catches what webhooks missed. This belt-and-suspenders pattern is what mature integrations actually run.

**When to skip webhooks and just poll:**
- The provider doesn't offer them.

- You need a guaranteed-complete snapshot rather than a stream of deltas.

**The real cost of webhooks** — budget for these or they'll bite you: you must verify signatures (treat the endpoint as attacker-reachable), handle retries and duplicate deliveries (make processing idempotent), respond fast and process async (don't do heavy work in the request handler or the provider will time out and retry), and handle out-of-order arrival.

**What would change this recommendation / where it's weakest.** This answer assumes a fairly standard integration; flip it if the facts differ. Concretely, I'd move off webhooks-primary if: the provider doesn't support webhooks or can't deliver them reliably (no retries, no signed payloads, poor delivery SLAs); you can't expose a reliable public endpoint; or you need completeness/ordering guarantees that an event stream can't give — there, a periodic full pull is the source of truth and webhooks become at best a latency optimization. The limitation to be honest about: webhooks don't remove polling, they just demote it — you still build and operate a reconciliation poller, so the "webhooks vs polling" framing is really "webhooks-plus-thin-polling vs polling-only," and if your volume and latency tolerance make the webhook infrastructure (endpoint, signature verification, idempotency, async processing, monitoring) cost more than it saves, polling-only is the correct call, not the fallback. Tell me the specific provider, your latency tolerance, and event volume and I'll firm this up.

The two decision factors that would most likely overturn my answer: does the provider support webhooks at all, and can you reliably expose an endpoint? If both are yes, go webhooks-primary. If either is no, poll.
P:
Recommendation: use webhooks as the primary mechanism, backed by a low-frequency reconciliation poll. Fall back to pure polling only when the provider offers no webhooks or you can't reliably expose a public endpoint.

**Why webhooks as the default.** They deliver updates the moment something changes, so you get near-real-time data without spending requests to ask "anything new?" Polling forces a trade-off you can't engineer away: poll often and most calls return "nothing changed" (wasting rate limit and cost), or poll rarely and accept latency. Webhooks remove the trade-off — you do work only when there's work to do. For the common cases (payments, CRM changes, deploy events, order status), that's the right shape.

**Why you keep a poll anyway.** Webhooks are fire-and-forget from the provider's side, so they fail silently. A dropped delivery, your endpoint being down during a deploy, or a transient network error means you simply never hear about an event. A reconciliation poll — "give me everything updated in the last N minutes," run every few minutes or hourly depending on your latency tolerance — catches whatever the webhooks missed. Two things make it reliable: key it off an `updated_since` cursor, and overlap the window generously (look back ~2× the interval) so an event near a boundary isn't lost. This belt-and-suspenders pattern is what mature integrations actually run; the webhook gives you speed, the poll gives you a completeness guarantee.

**One principle that simplifies the hard parts.** Treat a webhook as a signal that *something changed*, not as the source of truth, and have the handler fetch the current authoritative state from the provider's API. That one decision neutralizes out-of-order arrival, duplicate deliveries, and partial payloads at once — you always converge on the provider's latest state regardless of what order notifications land in. The exception is genuinely high-volume streams where the extra fetch per event is too expensive; there you trust the payload and handle ordering explicitly.

**Budget for the webhook costs up front, or they'll bite you:**
- **Verify signatures.** The endpoint is publicly reachable, so treat every request as attacker-controlled until the signature checks out.
- **Make processing idempotent.** Providers retry, so the same event will arrive more than once; dedupe on the event ID.
- **Respond fast, process async.** Acknowledge immediately and hand off to a queue. Heavy work in the request handler causes the provider to time out and retry, compounding load.
- **Dead-letter failed events.** When processing throws, park the event somewhere you can replay it, so you're not leaning solely on reconciliation to recover.

**When to skip webhooks and just poll:**
- The provider doesn't support them.
- You can't expose a stable public HTTPS endpoint (locked-down network, no clean ingress).
- Volume is tiny and latency genuinely doesn't matter — a daily batch sync is simpler than standing up an endpoint plus signature verification and retry handling.
- You need a guaranteed-complete snapshot rather than a stream of deltas.

The two factors that decide it: does the provider support webhooks, and can you reliably expose an endpoint? Both yes → webhooks-primary with a reconciliation poll. Either no → poll. Tell me the specific integration and your latency tolerance and I'll firm up the poll interval and the reconciliation window.
